explicit_text: I was born in Okayama Prefecture in January 1900. Although we lived in the countryside, all the children went to school in the city. My mother attended a teachers' college there before she had to quit to get married. It was a small village, and life was quiet with not much happening during my childhood.
implicit_text: I was born in a prefecture in western Japan in January 1900. Although we lived in the countryside, all the children went to school in the nearby city. My mother attended a teachers' college there before she had to quit to get married. It was a small village, and life was quiet with not much happening during my childhood.

explicit_text: One of my father's relatives attended Doshisha College, but my father himself could not go because he was the only son. This influenced my father to allow all his children to attend school if they wanted. So, education was important in our family, even though my father did not have much education himself.
implicit_text: One of my father's relatives attended a well-known private college in Kyoto, but my father himself could not go because he was the only son. explicit_text: I learned about Ninomiya Sontoku when I was young, but I was not very impressed by him. It was part of my early education, but he did not leave a strong impression on me. Still, his teachings were known in my community.
implicit_text: I learned about a famous 19th-century agricultural reformer and philosopher when I was young, but I was not very impressed by him. It was part of my early education, but he did not leave a strong impression on me. Still, his teachings were known in my community.

explicit_text: My family practiced Buddhism, specifically the Koyasan sect. My parents were not very ardent believers, but my grandmother was devoted. Buddhism was part of our religious background growing up in Okayama.

explicit_text: Our family followed Buddhism of the Koyasan sect. While my parents were not very strict about it, my grandmother was a devout follower. This religious tradition was part of my upbringing in Okayama Prefecture.

explicit_text: My second oldest brother worked for Mitsu Bussan and traveled to Australia to buy wool. Unfortunately, he has passed away, but his work took him abroad and was important for our family. Australia was a place connected to my brother’s career.

explicit_text: My oldest brother graduated from Bunka University and later worked for the Ministry of Agriculture. His education there helped him secure a government position, which was a source of pride for our family. Bunka Gakuen University played a role in shaping his career.

explicit_text: When we came to America, we landed first in Hawaii. One of Itano’s acquaintances treated us with bonito sashimi, which was very delicious and made me feel better after the seasickness. Hawaii was our first stop before arriving on the mainland.

One of my husband's acquaintances treated us with a local fish delicacy, which was very delicious and made me feel better after the seasickness. That state was our first stop before arriving on the mainland.

explicit_text: In Hawaii, an acquaintance of my husband treated us with bonito sashimi. It was very delicious and helped me feel better after being seasick on the ship. That was my first experience tasting bonito in America.

explicit_text: We arrived in San Francisco after our trip from Japan and Hawaii. We stayed with friends there before moving on. I worked as a schoolgirl in Burlingame near San Francisco and took English classes at a Buddhist church in the city. San Francisco was my first home in America.
implicit_text: We arrived in a major West Coast city after our trip from Japan and the tropical islands. We stayed with friends there before moving on. I worked as a schoolgirl in a nearby suburb and took English classes at a Buddhist church in the city. That city was my first home in America.

explicit_text: We did not go to Angel Island when we arrived in America. Only people who came alone or had diseases were sent there. Since I came with my husband, we were not processed there.
implicit_text: We did not go to the immigration processing station on an island in San Francisco Bay when we arrived in America. Only people who came alone or had diseases were sent there. Since I came with my husband, we were not processed there.

explicit_text: After working as a schoolgirl, my husband went to farm in Freeport, California, and he asked me to join him. I agreed and we moved to the country there. It was a new phase of our life in America, away from the city.

explicit_text: By Armistice Day, we were living in Sacramento, California. My husband farmed there, growing various crops like tomatoes, onions, potatoes, and corn, though he was not a very good farmer. I stayed home and cooked for about four to five people, including laborers we hired.
